reasoning: To achieve 16 consecutive weeks at No. 1 on the Billboard 200, an album must maintain steady, dominant sales for roughly four months. While Taylor Swift's recent album "The Tortured Poets Department" (TTPD) had a historic run, it tapped out at 12 *consecutive* weeks at No. 1 despite a massive debut and having higher 3rd-week units (282k) than "The Life of a Showgirl" (194k). Showgirl's units are dropping rapidly (down 43% in week 3) and it faces a notoriously competitive Q4 release window, meaning it will have to compete with November blockbusters and December's heavy surge of holiday albums. Given that even Swift's strongest modern albums haven't achieved 16 consecutive weeks (a feat rarely seen since the 1990s), and the fact that its sales are tracking lower than TTPD's at this point in its run, the likelihood of "The Life of a Showgirl" surviving exactly 16 uninterrupted weeks through the holiday season is extremely low. Thus, the probability of reaching 16 straight weeks is close to the minimum allowable probability.
